resources.string: Trigger conditions that require a Reader Action will only detect its selected Reader Action:
Valid Card — Raised when a valid credential belonging to a user is detected.
Valid PIN — Raised when a valid PIN belonging to a User is detected.
Single Badge — Raised when a user presents their credential for the first time, or after ~10 seconds. Subsequent consecutive badges that are part of the same action are not detected.
2-Badge Action — Raised when a user presents their credential twice in a row, triggering a 2-Badge Action. Does not trigger again if a 3-Badge Action is done.
3-Badge Action — Raised when a user presents their credential three times in a row, triggering a 3-Badge Action
This trigger condition will be true while the current system date and time matches an active time block in the selected time period.
This trigger condition will be true while the current system date and time does not match any active time blocks in the selected time period.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected output is on. Note that if the Invert Output State option is used for a Hardware Output, this trigger is based on the internal system state, not the physical relay state.

string.name: D15305D7A4E34E02489C74A5EF542F36
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "SiferVolume" entries

resources.string: This trigger condition will be true if the selected output is off. Note that if the Invert Output State option is used for a Hardware Output, this trigger is based on the internal system state, not the physical relay state.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected area is armed.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected area is disarmed.
This trigger condition will be true whenever entry delay is started by a user entering the selected area while it is armed, triggering an input that is assigned the
process group. The trigger condition will remain true for the duration of the entry delay which is configured in that area or until the area is disarmed.

This trigger condition will be true whenever a user arms an area and triggers the exit delay which defines the amount of time they have to exit the area. The trigger condition will remain true for the duration of the exit delay which is configured in that area. Note that exit delay is only triggered on LCD Terminal arming or using the web interface option
This trigger condition will be true whenever an alarm is triggered in the selected area, and will stay true until the area is disarmed.

This trigger condition will be true as long as the area has at least one active alarm of the chosen Filter to Alarm Type that has not yet restored. Note that this only relates to active alarms, for example, an intruder detector detecting motion in a disarmed area would not trigger this condition.

This trigger condition will be true whenever there is at least one active input in the selected area, staying true until there are no active inputs in the area. This occurs regardless of the area arm state. If triggering actions on each input activation is required, the new activation may be detected with the condition 'Detecting New Input Activity (Pulsed)' instead.

This trigger condition will pulse whenever an input in the selected area becomes active. This occurs regardless of the area arm state. This triggers for every individual input activation in the area even when another input is still currently active.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected area has been configured with multi-mode arming and has been armed into the Full Area mode, turning false when no longer armed or armed in a different mode.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected area has been configured with multi-mode arming and has been armed into the Perimeter Mode mode, turning false when no longer armed or armed in a different mode.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected area has been configured with multi-mode arming and has been armed into the Night Mode mode, turning false when no longer armed or armed in a different mode.
This trigger condition will be true while the area is in the 'Arm Warning' state, defined by the option

. The arm warning phase occurs prior to an area automatically arming by the system, allowing users time to exit the area or cancel the arm. The auto-arming can occur because of area inactivity, scheduling, or defer area arming. This condition remains true until the warning time has elapsed and the area arms, or the arm warning phase is cancelled.
This trigger condition will be true for a few seconds after a successful area arm attempt.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever the reporting process successfully sends out any messages for closings in this area. Note that the area's
must be configured to report closings for this trigger condition to be possible. This trigger can be used to show a visual or audible response that the close event was successfully reported.
This trigger condition will be true for a few seconds after an unsuccessful area arm attempt.

This trigger condition will be true if an Area Walk Test has been started through the web interface for the selected area, and will become false when the Area Walk Test has been cancelled or is successfully completed. Walk test mode is automatically cancelled if the area is armed while the test is running.

This trigger condition will be true if the number of users in the area meets the chosen comparison to the Number of Users value, and will turn back to false if it no longer meets this condition after the number of users in the area changes again.

string.name: D0F2E5376298C880665077B565FFD7DD
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "LockStates" entries

resources.string: This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently locked.

string.name: C76FD517E45CC95709F4AC106EFA4A94
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "LockStates" entries

resources.string: This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently unlocked.

string.name: C3BF447EABE632720A3AA1A7CE401274
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "DoorStates" entries

resources.string: This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently open.

string.name: 03F4A47830F97377A35321051685071E
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "DoorStates" entries

resources.string: This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently closed.

This trigger condition will be true whenever the door is placed into the Lockout state, preventing users being granted access to the door. This trigger condition will last until the lockout is cleared and the door has reverted back to its normal behaviour.

This trigger condition will be true when the door is opened without the door being unlocked first, causing the door to enter the 'forced' state. This trigger condition will stay true until the door closes or is unlocked.

This trigger condition will be true when the door is in the 'Held Open Warning' phase. This occurs when the door locks while still open, and remains that way for the Held (DOTL) Time, entering a warning phase for Held (DOTL) Warning Time. This condition allows local responses to close the door before it is officially held open too long and alarm events are reported, etc. If the door closes, the door is unlocked again, or if that warning time expires and the door becomes 'Held Open', this trigger condition ends.

This trigger condition will be true when the door is in the 'Held Open Too Long' phase, following a door remaining open after locking for the Held (DOTL) Time and Held (DOTL) Warning Time. The trigger condition will remain true until the door is closed or it is unlocked.
This trigger condition will be true when any readers linked to the Door have detected reader tampering, where it will remain true until the tampering has been resolved.

This trigger condition will pulse whenever a user is denied access for the selected door. This could be due to insufficient permissions or the door being in a Locked Out state. Note that this applies for known and unknown users requesting access to the door. Unknown credentials or inactive credentials do trigger this condition.
This trigger condition will be true whenever the wireless door reports a Low Battery state to the Inception system.
This trigger condition will be true when communication is lost between the wireless lock and the hub, or the hub and the module.

This trigger condition will be true while the lift floor is secured, which requires a card to be presented in a lift car before a button linked to the floor would be enabled. When the lift floor is Locked Out it is also Secured.
This trigger condition will be true while the lift floor is in the Free Access state.
This trigger condition will be true while the lift floor has been locked out and will only turn false when this state has been overridden.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is secured.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is unlocked.
This trigger condition will be true if the storage unit is still unlocked after a time period that can be defined by the
option in the block that the unit is associated with. This trigger condition will become false again after the unit is locked again.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is currently open.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is currently closed.

This trigger condition will be true whenever an alarm is triggered for the selected storage unit, which occurs when there is an active input within a secured unit. This state will remain true until all inputs in that unit are no longer active or if the unit unlocks or is marked as vacant.

This trigger condition will be true whenever tamper is triggered for the selected storage unit, which occurs when a detector within the Unit is tampered with. This state will remain true until all inputs in that unit are no longer tampered.
This trigger condition will be true as long as the storage unit has been marked vacant, and will only turn false again if the vacancy has been cleared.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is in the secure pending state, due to it having one or more unsealed inputs.
This trigger condition will be true while all storage units in the chosen storage block are currently secure.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block that are currently unlocked.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block that are currently in the state of being unlocked too long after a time period defined by the
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block are currently in alarm.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block are currently in tamper.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block that are pending secure due to unsealed inputs.

string.name: 7A2CCF251ECB20B2B84CE0E3C3F72A29
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "UnitStates" entries

resources.string: This trigger condition will be true as long as the chosen input is secure.
This trigger condition will be true as long as the chosen input is active.
This trigger condition will be true as long as the chosen input has been isolated, and will only turn false when de-isolated.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever a received message from the connection matches the defined message value of this condition.

This trigger condition is true while the selected connection is enabled and is able to use the configured details to retain a connection. Note that other states such as Connecting or Disabled are not considered to be Connected.
This trigger condition is true while the selected connection has been enabled but is Disconnected, has failed to connect with the given configuration, or is in the process of connecting.
Detecting Reader Action by Any User (Pulsed)
This trigger condition will pulse whenever any user performs the selected Reader Action at the selected reader.
Detecting Reader Action by Specific User (Pulsed)
This trigger condition will pulse whenever the selected User performs the selected Reader Action at the chosen reader.
Detecting Reader Action by User with Permission Group (Pulsed)

This trigger condition will pulse whenever any user with the given Permission Group performs the selected Reader Action at the selected reader. This trigger allows more generic conditions to be defined, such as triggering if any Manager badges. It also allows the condition to remain up to date as users are added to or removed from the system.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever an invalid or unknown card has been detected at the selected reader.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever an invalid PIN has been entered at the selected reader.

string.name: 8D9DA4BC0E49A50E09AC9F7E56789D39
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "ModuleHealthStates" entries

resources.string: This trigger condition will be true while the selected Hardware Module is offline. This condition applies to any expansion module.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected cabinet tampering, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion module with a dedicated Cabinet Tamper input.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected internal siren tampering, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to expansion modules with dedicated Internal Siren drivers.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected external siren tampering, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to expansion modules with dedicated External Siren drivers.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected that the AC Power is missing, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a low battery, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a missing battery, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a shorted wire across power output terminals, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a failed power supply, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the latest battery test on the selected Hardware Module has failed, remaining true until another successful battery test (automatic or manual) is completed. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected that module substitution has occurred, remaining true while a module substitution is still being detected. This condition can apply to any expansion module.
This trigger condition will pulse when a failed PIN attempt has been detected by the hardware module. This condition can apply only to Terminal expansion modules.

This trigger condition will pulse when user duress has been detected by the hardware module. This condition can apply to Terminal expansion modules or SLAMs with keypad readers attached. The event is raised if the Duress User PIN is entered at a keypad reader or a terminal, or if [HELP] [HELP] [HELP] is pressed at a Terminal module.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected RF interference or jamming, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply only to RF Input expansion modules.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected tampering on a door reader, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any door expansion modules.
This trigger condition will be true if the selected counter's value meets the chosen comparison to the counter value option, and will turn back to false if it no longer meets this condition.
This trigger condition will be true if the selected Integrated Connection encounters a connection error, and will turn back to false when this connection error is resolved.

resources.string: The 4-hex-digit Client Code assigned to this site.

The area (partition) reporting id used when reporting system health events like System Resets, Cabinet Tamper, Module Offline, AC Fail etc. This should be unique across all other area reporting ids in the system. This has the same function as the 'system area' within Concept 4000 and Integriti systems
The Repeat Alarm Event Limit or

string.name: 385041109D12A08EDB0F50398704842D
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "ReportingPacketType" entries

resources.string: is a feature designed to limit the number of repeated input activations or module health issues that are sent to a monitoring station. Often, this option is used in case a detector device becomes faulty and begins repeatedly activating, preventing repeated false signals from being sent through. It can also be used to stop an intruder from intentionally activating an input many times and masking their movements in the flood of input events from that one input.
If the limit is set to zero, then the feature is disabled and no limit is applied.

In addition to input events, module health issues are also tracked and are affected by the repeat event limit. For module events, this can prevent events like Low Battery from sending repeatedly if the battery capacity is on the edge of detection.

The system tracks individual input events and module health events towards the repeat event limit, identifying each event separately. In this case, repeated alarm events from an input can trigger swinger shutdown and stopping future alarm events from that input from being sent, but if a tamper event is then detected on that input then it will still be reported. Similarly, a module's low battery event could be limited, but a cabinet tamper or AC fail event from that module is different and hence tracked and limited separately and will still be sent through.

Once the event limit is reached for an input or module health event, a separate event is sent to the monitoring station for this input or module to identify this. All new activations for that input or module health issue will then be discarded and will continue until an area disarm. For inputs, the limit is reset when the area that includes the input is disarmed. For module health events, as there is no area that they belong to, any area disarm will reset the limits.
Prevents the Technician On Site event from being reported. Some monitoring stations don't handle the event well, so the event can be disabled if it is causing issues.

If enabled, more detailed or additional events around area arm/disarm status will be reported to monitoring stations. This extra information is needed for standards compliance in certain regions such as UL and ULC Intruder standards.
- If an area is armed with isolated inputs, a Partial Arm event will be reported instead of a standard Close.
- Disarming an area during an Arm Warning phase (cancelling the pending arm) will report a User on Premises event.
- An area arm attempt by a user that fails due to unsealed inputs will result in a Failed to Close event being reported.
Note that this option is only available with ContactID reporting and should not be used with other reporting formats.
If enabled, the review event that triggers on alarm when there is no reporting path configured will be suppressed. This option is intended for situations where Notifiers (configurable at
) are used to send alarm reporting events via email or push notification for self-monitoring purposes, and no monitoring station is being used.

Enables reporting of events and alarms through Skytunnel. Skytunnel is an industry first, cloud-based service that provides hassle free connection and reporting over the internet. Selecting this option will enable the

Note, this only enables the reporting path. Your Inception must also be enrolled onto your monitoring stations Multipath IP system using the Inception's serial number. Contact your monitoring station in order to set up this account, you will require the device type (Inception), the serial number and the reporting format you wish to use.
This option is used to determine the format that Skytunnel alarm messages will be reported with.

Contact ID: A reporting format is used to report area opens/closes and zone/input isolates, tampers, alarms and restores using the Ademco contact id format. A Contact ID Report can be generated from the toolbox above. This report contains all possible events that can be reported based on the system configuration and can be provided to the monitoring station or used as reference material.

IR-Fast: A proprietary, Inner Range reporting format that can report area opens/closes and zone/input isolations, tampers, alarms and restores to a central station. Unlike contact id, no input or user mapping is required because all alarm reports and events can be reported uniquely.
IR-Fast with text: This provides the additional functionality of sending text with any reported events or alarms. The full text description of the event will be included.

If enabled, this option will result in a test message periodically being sent to the monitoring station. A test report is used to confirm the system's capability to send events and alarms to a monitoring station and the monitoring station will expect messages at a regular frequency. The frequency chosen below should match how often the monitoring station expects to receive test reports

If the test report is not received by the monitoring station, this may indicate an issue with the Inception Controller, unwanted programming changes or issues with the reporting paths. Selecting this option will enable the

This will determine the rate at which test reports will be sent to the monitoring station. Select one of the available options from the dropdown box. The frequency chosen should match how often the monitoring station expects to receive test reports
This will determine what time the test report will be sent to the monitoring station.

Enables reporting alarms through the T4000 module or other supported third-party alarm devices. The T4000 security communicator is a highly advanced device used to connect the Inception controller to a central monitoring station via multiple highly secure IP paths. Selecting this option will enable the

Note, this only enables the reporting path. Your T4000 must also be enrolled onto your monitoring stations Multipath IP system using the T4000 serial number. Contact your monitoring station in order to set up this account, you will require the device type (T4000) and the serial number of your T4000. The reporting format is selected by the monitoring station.
See the T4000 installation guide for more information in setting up this device.

Note that if using the IR-Fast with Text format, then area and input names within the system should be kept short as there is a limit on the number of characters that can be transmitted. There is a limit of 48 characters which must include the area and input names, along with the alarm event name.
Select the type of device you are reporting alarms with from the available supported types. Depending on the device type chosen, different configuration options may become available below.
Determines the serial port that the alarm device should try and connect to. Choosing
will cause the device to attempt to connect to the first plugged-in and unassigned serial device.
If using multiple serial connections, it is recommended that a serial connection be specified
Sets the maximum amount of time the T4000 should try and send an alarm before timing out.
Note: Once timed out, Inception can attempt to send the event via another alarm path. If all enabled paths fail, Inception will continue to retry the send on each path for 15 minutes.

If the T4000 is configured for IRFast alarm reporting, then enabling this option will cause Inception to report IRFast alarms in Integriti-compatible format, rather than the legacy Concept-compatible format (default). The Integriti IRFast format adds support for reporting additional input state types that are not supported by the Concept IRFast protocol, e.g. Low Battery and Poll Failed.
This option is used to determine the format that alarm messages will be reported with via the alarm communication unit. This should match the format that the alarm monitoring station is expecting.

Both Contact ID and SIA reporting formats report the same types of events in similar formats, primarily including information about the client account, the event code and the triggering point ID. SIA is capable of including an additional text description about the event, while Contact ID provides more coded information about the event such as the area where the event occured in.

A commissioning report can be generated for either format via the toolbox above that can be sent to the monitoring station to be imported into their system. This provides detailed text descriptions of all possible events that can be reported by the system based on the the system configuration, along with the triggering event codes and point information. With this information, the monitoring station can convert the coded alarm events into clear and useful alarm information.
The IP address of the central station running the receiver software. This will be the address where all alarms are sent and where regular polling is configured.

The account number or client code that identifies this device at the monitoring station. Depending on the monitoring station that is being communicated with, the account number should be four or more characters long.
If required, the APN to allow the device to access the GPRS network.
If required, the username to allow the device to access the GPRS network.
If required, the password to allow the device to access the GPRS network.
The country that the dialler is installed in. Required when connected to a standard phone line in order to dial correctly.
The phone number of the alarm monitoring station's receiver that should be dialled.
If connected to a universal transmitter with dialler capture, phone number may still be required.

By default, the modem attempts to keep the line open after establishing a connection and sending an alarm. This allows multiple alarms to be sent in a single dial, maximising the speed at which alarms can be reported, and is especially useful when connected to a universal transmitter via dialler capture.

If the modem is connected to a standard phone line that is shared with other devices though (such as a fax machine), this option will cause the modem to hang up the line after sending an alarm. Note that this means each alarm requires a dial attempt, which can drastically slow down the rate at which alarms can be reported.

If enabled, this option will result in a test message periodically being sent to the monitoring station. A test report is used to confirm the system's capability to send events and alarms to a monitoring station and the monitoring station will expect messages at a regular frequency. The frequency chosen below should match how often the monitoring station expects to receive test reports.

Determines the rate that test reports are sent to the monitoring station. Select one of the available options from the dropdown box. The frequency chosen should match how often the monitoring station expects to receive test reports
Determines what time the test report will be sent to the monitoring station.
Determines if the Skytunnel reporting path should be used as the Primary Path or as a Backup Path in the case that multiple paths are being used.
Alarm events will always attempt to be sent via the primary path and will only fall back to the backup path if sending fails.
Determines if the alarm device reporting path should be used as the Primary Path or as a Backup Path in the case that multiple paths are being used.
Determines if the SIA IP reporting path should be used as the Primary Path or as a Backup Path in the case that multiple paths are being used.

resources.string: Determines how the alarm device reports its Path Status state. Alarm devices can report their path status directly through a high-level integration, or through toggling the state of one or more inputs. The alarm device you are using will determine which option should be selected. NOTE: Changes in Path Status will only log review events when the alarm device is currently connected to Inception.
- The high-level integration for the alarm device reports the Path Status directly.

- The alarm device reports its Path Status by controlling the state of a hardware output to represent when paths are available. This device output should be connected to Inception as an input and selected in the Path Status Input field.
Specifies the input whose state should be used to determine the path status of the alarm device. By default, when the input is in an alarm state it represents
, but this behaviour can be reversed with the

string.name: A386AE356CFC385C3C75ADA58B130F27
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "SwapOptions" entries

resources.string: The entry delay is the duration of time that the user has to disarm an area without generating alarms. The entry delay is started when a user enters an armed area and triggers an input that is assigned the
process group. It is recommended that the entry delay duration be long enough for a user to reach a keypad and disarm an area.
The exit delay is the duration of time that the user has to exit the area after arming the area. Inputs that are assigned the
process groups form an exit path and, if triggered, will not generate an area alarm during the exit delay.
It is recommended that the exit delay duration be long enough for a user to successfully exit the building.
The exit delay is only triggered when an area is armed from an LCD Terminal or the web interface via the
Temporary disarm is a feature that allows a user to disarm an area, with the area automatically re-arming at the end of the

time. Temporary disarming, or defer arm, is often used to ensure that an area is not accidentally left disarmed when the user leaves the site. The temporary disarm feature is commonly used for users who access an area for a short period of time, such as a cleaner, or by someone who is accessing the area after hours.
If the temporary disarm feature is being used in the area, it is recommended to use the
option below, as this alerts users that the area will re-arm soon. This option provides the user with time to either leave the area or disarm the area a second time.

A user does not need to perform a special action to temporarily disarm an area, instead, the decision for whether an area is disarmed normally or temporarily disarmed is based on the user's permissions. To allow a user to disarm an area temporarily, assign to the user permission to control the area with the

. When the user requests to disarm the area, the user is first checked to see if they have permission to disarm the area normally. If not, they are then checked to see if they have permission to temporarily disarm the area. This allows normal disarm permissions to take precedence. This occurs with any disarm request from the user, be it from a terminal, the web interface, card swipe or remote fob button press.
Temporary disarm can be used with the

option below to ensure the area is not left disarmed overnight. A user could be given permission to disarm the area normally throughout the day, but then only given permission to temporarily disarm the area at night. If the user stays back after the auto-arm scheduled time, when the user cancels the automatic arm the area will only be temporarily disarmed, ensuring that once they do leave, the area will arm in case they forget.

time, it is recommended to choose a delay that is longer than what the user would typically need. For example, selecting two hours if the cleaner normally takes an hour and a half. This prevents the user from needing to repeatedly disarm the area each time the delay time was reached, while still allowing them to stay longer than the time if required on rare occasions by disarming again.

Provides an additional period of time before an area automatically arms to allow users to exit or manually disarm the area. The auto-arming of an area can occur because of area inactivity, scheduling or defer area arming. The auto-arm warn time can be used to generate an audible warning tone on sirens, siren outputs, readers and keypads. This allows the user time to either leave the area, or disarm the area to cancel the arm.

The reporting ID is the group or area number identifier used by the various reporting formats that Inception supports. The area reporting ID is necessary for most control rooms to identify and interpret area open/closes and alarms. The system automatically generates these id's each time a new area is created.

It is possible to manually assign ID's, however in most circumstances this is unnecessary. Ensure that if you are assigning ID's manually that they are unique across all areas in the system, that is, no two areas share the same ID number. This also includes the system health area reporting ID located under

resources.string: Determines how the open (disarm) and close (arm) events are sent to monitoring stations.
- The area will generate a report for both events.
- Reduces the overall opening reports sent by only sending openings if an alarm has occurred in the area.
- no open and close reporting for this area will occur.

Determines which input alarm events are reported to a monitoring station when they occur. Note that this only affects the reporting of input events, any siren responses will still occur regardless of the option chosen.
- All input alarms are reported. This is the default option and will be the one most commonly used.

input types) will not be sent. All other event types (including tamper events from intruder inputs) such as emergency, smoke, duress and environmental input alarms will still be reported. Commonly used when people will be on site near the armed area, such as a section of a building or if a downstairs area was armed at home. If an intruder event occurs, the sirens will sound to attract attention and allow investigation, but the monitoring station will not be notified.
- No input alarm events will be reported for this area. Siren responses will still occur depending on the input type.
Allows inputs to be added or removed from the area. Once an input is added you can assign the input an
to determine how the area will process the inputs state changes. The
page provides a simplified process for adding inputs to areas.
- No sirens or strobes are enabled, no alarms are reported. Used for automation purposes, an automated action can have an area trigger condition of
which this along with all other input types would trigger.

string.name: 8B85DDF1385616518E234664AE047577
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "InputTypeNotifications" entries

resources.string: - When the input is triggered, alarm siren tones are played on internal sirens only, strobes are enabled and a door forced event is sent to the monitoring station. The alarm response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. Used on doors that should only be opened when unlocked, such as after an access request or REX button. Best used with the

calculated input that is dynamically created when a door has a reed or tongue input assigned to it. Note that doors with free egress (e.g an external door that can be opened from the inside without a REX button or
the door) should not be monitored with this input type, as the door will be

- When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes are enabled, but a door held open event is sent to the monitoring station. The event will be sent regardless of the area's arm state. Used as an escalation option when important doors are held open too long. A warning phase can occur with local buzzers to attract attention so that the door can be closed, but if no one is nearby and that phase passes then an event can be sent, allowing the monitoring station to contact someone to ensure the door is closed. Best used with the
calculated input that is dynamically created when a door has a reed or tongue input assigned to it.

- When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes are enabled, but a duress event is sent to the monitoring station. The duress event will be sent regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.

string.name: A3FA706F20BC0B7858B7AE6932261940
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "InputTypeNotifications" entries

resources.string: - When the input is triggered, evacuation siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and an emergency event is sent to the monitoring station. The emergency response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.

string.name: 1B531AB76343253F9E0F23228ACAEE0A
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "InputTypeNotifications" entries

resources.string: - When the input is triggered, no sirens or strobes will be activated. However, a non-burglary event will be sent to the monitoring station. The non-burglary response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. Used to represent non-intruder alarms like temperature alarms or water sensors to notify the monitoring station or other users of the event. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.

- When the input is triggered with the area armed and no entry or exit delay active, alarm sirens are played, strobes are enabled and an intruder event is sent to the monitoring station. If disarmed, the alarm responses will not occur. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state

- Reports with the same alarm event type as a Duress input type, however alarm sirens are played and the area goes into alarm. Hold up events are processed regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state

- When the input is triggered with the area armed, alarm sirens are played, strobes are enabled and an intruder event is sent to the monitoring station. If disarmed, the alarm responses will not occur. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state

string.name: 077262CC53A1FB1B5F651D31B6BF81BA
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "InputTypeNotifications" entries

resources.string: - When the input is triggered, alarm siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and a medical event is sent to the monitoring station. The medical response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.

- When the input is triggered, if the area is armed and no alarms are active, Entry Delay mode will begin. If alarms are active or the area is not disarmed before entry delay ends, then alarm siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and an intruder event is sent to the monitoring station. If disarmed, the alarm responses will not occur. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state.

string.name: BC93FD0FA19ED26B6ADF8293ADA55404
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "InputTypeNotifications" entries

resources.string: - When the input is triggered, fire evacuation siren tones are played, strobes are enabled and a smoke event is sent to the monitoring station. The smoke response will occur regardless of the area's arm state. If tampered with, the same response will occur, except with a tamper event being sent to the monitoring station instead.

- When the input is triggered, alarm sirens are played and the area goes into alarm, regardless of the area's arm state. However, intruder events are only reported if the area is armed. Used to represent a Storage Unit alarm, where sirens can sound during the day for unauthorised unit access while staff are present, while sirens and event reporting can occur after hours when the area is armed. If tampered with, area sirens occur and a tamper event will be sent to the monitoring station. This tamper response would occur regardless of the area's arm state

inputs for Wireless Doors, the corresponding *Low Battery* or *Poll Failed* input event will be sent to the monitoring station, regardless of the area's arm state. However, no strobes or sirens will activate. This provides a way for the various Wireless Door states to be reported without interfering with the standard intruder reporting patterns. Note that inputs that are not
are not handled by this process group.

If enabled, this area can be armed into several different modes. This replaces the basic Arm mode, allowing the area to be armed in Full (Away), Perimeter (Stay) or Night (Instant) modes. These modes are commonly used in home scenarios, where the exterior of the building should be armed while people are inside. As with the standard arm mode, only the intruder related input types are affected. Inputs like Smoke and Duress will be monitored regardless of the arm mode.
The arm modes have the following behaviour:
- Exactly the same behaviour as the standard Arm mode. The whole area is armed with Exit Delay if required. Intended for use when everyone is leaving the area.
- Arms only the perimeter of the area. Intended for use when people will remain in the area, securing only the perimeter of the building. Only the perimeter inputs are monitored (specified in the
option below). If a Primary input activates, entry delay will begin (e.g someone opens a door).
- Intended for use when people will remain in the area, but no one is expected to enter or leave. The perimeter inputs and interior night inputs are monitored (specified in the

option below). If any perimeter input activates (including Primary inputs), the alarm will activate immediately. This allows warning if someone breaks into the front door while everyone is asleep for example. Optionally, additional interior night inputs can be specified to monitor more of the area in this mode. These interior inputs are treated as Primary inputs, triggering entry delay (and hence not the sirens immediately) if someone activates those inputs.

option below becomes available, where the inputs that should be monitored in Perimeter and Night modes. These would be the inputs such as window or door reed switches and so on that protect potential entry points to the area.

option also becomes available, which allows additional inputs (and hence more of the building) to be monitored during Night mode. These would be inputs monitoring locations where people should not go when the building is armed for the night, such as downstairs or living areas while users are asleep.

To prevent accidental alarms during Perimeter mode, any perimeter inputs that may be commonly opened (doors, certain windows etc) can be set as Primary inputs. This will cause entry delay to begin in Perimeter mode and give a chance for the area to be disarmed. In Instant mode though, they will still go straight into alarm.
This is optional per-area, so you can have a multi-mode House area, but a standard arm Garage and Workshop area for example.
option above is enabled. This option allows the perimeter intruder inputs of the area to be specified.
When this area is armed in Perimeter or Night mode, only the intruder inputs (Primary, Handover, Instant) specified in this list will be monitored. The inputs in this list must also appear in the
list above. Note that non-intruder input types (Smoke, Duress etc) don't need to be included in this list, they will still be monitored regardless of the arm mode.

These inputs typically represent the intruder detection points that protect the perimeter of an area, such as door and window reed switches. Perimeter and Night arm modes would be used when people are inside the area, so internal motion detectors generally would not be included in this list.
option above is enabled. This option allows additional inputs to be monitored during the Night (Instant) arm mode.

When this area is armed in Night mode, the intruder inputs (Primary, Handover, Instant) specified in this list will also be monitored in addition to the Perimeter inputs. Any interior night inputs are treated as Primary inputs, triggering Entry Delay if activated. This allows more of the building to be monitored while everyone is asleep, while not immediately activating sirens if someone enters that section.
The inputs in this list must also appear in the

The pulse count determines the number of input alarm activations required before an area alarm is triggered. Activations are counted from both the same input triggering multiple times or from different inputs within the area, and activations must occur within the
time to trigger the alarm. Once an alarm has been triggered, pulse counting is stopped and each subsequent activation is processed normally.

Input pulse counting is often used to help reduce false alarms from being reported, allowing once-off activations to be ignored while repeat activations are processed. False alarms can occur for a wide variety of reasons, with some common examples being pets moving around or wind from a climate control system or a storm blowing items and triggering motion detection or vibration sensors.

Pulse counting only affects alarm events from Primary, Handover or Instant Alarm inputs. Other input type events such as Duress, Smoke Detection, Medical or Emergency will all be processed normally. Similarly, any input tamper events will also be processed normally.

When choosing a value for the pulse count it is important to consider the environment this area is protecting. A high-security environment would require a lower pulse count in order to trigger sooner, while a location with pets or a harsh environment may require a higher count to filter out occasional activations.
The window of time where a number of input alarm activations must occur before an alarm is reported. The number of activations is configured in

In addition to pulse counting, an area alarm will also be triggered if an activation is detected that remains active for longer than the pulse duration without restoring. This prevents pulse counting from being bypassed by an input activating but never restoring.

One or more inputs can be selected that are used to cut an active exit delay short. These are often used to prevent long exit delays from running for the full duration when the user has already left the area.

Commonly, the primary input for the area or the exit door reed switch or tongue sense is used, or a button on the outside of the building to ensure that the user has left the area before cutting exit delay short. The input(s) specified do not need to be inputs that are monitored by the area, any inputs in the system can be used.

To activate the feature, any of the inputs specified need to transition from an active state to a secure state. A small delay is built in when processing the input that caused the exit delay to complete to prevent false alarms. This is to ensure that the input has settled incase, for example, the input is a door reed switch or tongue sense that could bounce briefly when the door closes.
Ignoring of module health issues for this area affects two main features in the system:

- By default, if a module indicates a tamper issue (such as siren or cabinet tampers), all areas in the system will generate an alarm response by sounding sirens and activating strobes. This may be undesirable on non-security areas as they would need to be disarmed. Enabling of this option causes this area to be excluded from the automatic tamper response.
Verify System Health on Area Arm

page causes area arm attempts to check for any module health issues or alarms comms issues in the system. These are automatically overridden if the system arms the area, however if a user arms the area the arm attempt can fail. This allows the user to either fix the issue before reattempting the arm or overriding the issue and continuing with the arm. If this behaviour is not desired for this area, there are two options to avoid this. First, enabling the
option will prevent this area from testing system health items when arming. Alternatively, the
Auto-Isolate Unsealed Inputs on User Arm
section below can be used to cause those issues to be automatically overidden.
Include Exit Path Inputs in Seal Check

Determines whether Handover/Exit Path Inputs are included in the seal check when attempting to arm an Area with Exit Delay. By default, exit path inputs are ignored in the pre-arm seal check when exit delay is active. Enabling this option will cause arm attempts with Exit Delay to fail if any Exit Path Inputs are active during the check, and prompt the user to isolate or re-seal the input(s) before trying to arm the Area again.
Cancel Alarm State on All Inputs Secure
When this is enabled, securing all active inputs within an area will clear the alarm state. Following this, an
event will be sent to the monitoring station, and all sirens and strobes will be stopped.

This new option is specifically designed for areas that are monitoring latched inputs, where another action is required to secure the input (duress inputs or emergency exit buttons with a manual release, storage unit alarm inputs with
Clear Unit Alarm when Inputs Seal
option is deselected on a Storage Block, this area option is enabled and the calculated input of a storage unit monitored by the area triggers an alarm, the storage unit owner can either execute an
action on their unit. The area in charge of monitoring the storage unit's input will then automatically restore its alarms, transmit an

event to the monitoring station, and deactivate all strobes and sirens. This removes the need for a site administrator to manually access the system, disarm the area, and rearm it to resume input monitoring.
It is recommended that Contact ID is used for alarm reporting when using this option, as there is a corresponding event type defined in this protocol.
Determines which modules will activate siren drivers when a siren is triggered. To add a siren device click the

button, this will open all of the available siren devices. Only modules with the S1INT (internal) and S2EXT (external) outputs can drive 8 ohm horn sirens, for example, the 8 Zone LAN Expander. All Input Types (Process Groups) that are capable of triggering a siren will trigger both internal and external siren outputs.
In addition to generating siren tones, the siren devices can be used to provide audible feedback for area arm success, arm fail, auto arm warnings, entry delay and exit delay.
Assign siren devices to this area.

Determines which modules will activate the Siren S1INT and S2EXT outputs when an Input programmed to generate Sirens is triggered. Will also determine which Siren Outputs will activate for the Area Arm Success, Area Arm Fail and Auto Arm warnings; as well as the Entry Delay and Exit Delay if siren activation is required.

Determines which additional outputs will turn on when a siren is triggered, this does not require the siren device above to be programmed. The siren output is commonly used to control piezo screamers. Once a siren output is defined and its location is set (internal or external), it will operate as per a siren device of the same type. To add a siren output, click the
button, this will open a window containing all the available outputs.
The duration of time that the siren will be activated. The siren will stop if a user logs into a keypad or disarms the area.
Note: Your local regulations will determine the maximum time a siren can sound.
If configured, an additional option will appear to allow a custom alarm response location.
If not configured, the location will be inferred from the input location.
Determines if the internal, external or both sirens should sound on alarm.
Determines if the internal, external or both sirens sound briefly to indicate the successful arming of the area.
Determines if the internal, external or both sirens sound briefly to indicate the failed arming of the area.
Determines if the internal, external or both sirens sound for the duration of the entry delay.
Determines if the internal, external or both sirens sound for the duration of the exit delay.
Determines if the internal, external or both sirens sound for the auto-arm warning of the area.
Set the maximum number of times that this area is able to activate the siren. If this is set at 0, then activations will be unlimited. Disarming the area resets this counter.

Determines which outputs will activate to control strobes. Any input type that is capable of strobe control will turn the strobe output on, this output will remain on until the area is disarmed. To add a strobe output click the
button, this will display a window containing all the available outputs. You can add as many strobe outputs as required.
Assign strobe outputs to be triggered when the area's siren(s) sound.
Determines which Output or Outputs are physically wired to a Strobe or will behave like a Strobe. These outputs will be activated by an area alarm and will turn off once the area is disarmed.

If enabled, all strobe outputs will turn on for three seconds when the area is successfully armed. This is commonly used when arming an area via a remote or reader to give the user a visual indication that the arm succeeded.

If enabled, all strobe outputs will turn on for six seconds when the area fails to arm. This is commonly used when arming an area via a remote or reader to give the user a visual indication that the arm failed and they need to investigate why.

A time period can be selected to automatically arm the area. This can be used to arm an area late at night in case a user forgets to arm it. The area will automatically arm when the time period goes valid (start of the time period).
is recommended to be used with the
option above, which can alert users that the area will arm soon. This allows the user time to either leave the area, or disarm the area to cancel the arm.
Note: the area won't try to re-arm if the scheduled arm is canceled. If being used to arm the area late at night to ensure it is armed properly overnight, it is recommended to be used with the
functionality to reduce the chance of the area being left unsecure when the user leaves.
option above is used, the area will automatically arm when the time period becomes valid. This is often used to ensure an area is armed at night incase a user forgets.

section above), the area can be armed in Full (Away), Perimeter (Stay) or Night (Instant) modes. This option determines which mode the area is armed into when the schedule becomes valid. This could be set for late at night for example, to ensure the area is put into Night mode.
If enabled, the area will automatically disarm when the time period selected in the auto-arm schedule goes inactive (end of the time period).

Caution: this option could leave the area unsecure at an undesired time if time periods aren't managed properly. For example, the area could disarm on a public holiday when no one is around, leaving the area unprotected.

The Auto-Arm Inactivity Time Schedule field is an optional field that can be used to restrict the time that the Auto-Arm Inactivity Time field's logic is active, for example: you may want inactivity auto-arm to only occur outside of working hours. The inactivity time will be counted from the time the schedule becomes active, for example: if the schedule becomes active at 7pm and the Inactivity Time is set to 30 minutes, the area will only be able to arm at 7:30pm at the earliest. If no schedule is set, the auto-arm inactivity logic will always be active.

The auto-arm inactivity time allows the area to be auto-armed if no activity is detected on the inputs within the area for a given period of time. This is commonly used to auto-arm rarely used areas without needing user interaction, ensuring they are secured more often. To set an auto-arm inactivity time, select the duration of time from the drop-down list.

The optional Auto-Arm Inactivity Time Schedule field can be used to restrict the times that the auto-arm inactivity logic is active. If no schedule is set, the auto-arm inactivity logic will always be active.
option is recommended to be used with the

option above, which can alert users that the area will arm soon. This allows the user time to either leave the area, or disarm the area to cancel the arm. Once the warning time begins, activity on the inputs will not cancel the arm, it can only be stopped by disarming the area.
Allow Input Activity to Cancel Auto-Arm Warning
If enabled, area inputs will automatically cancel an active Arm Warning state if they detect activity. The
option below determines the type of action that will be performed to cancel the area arm.

This option allows a user in the area to cancel an upcoming area arm without having to walk to a terminal or reader to do so. For example: a cleaner on the premises after hours may activate a motion sensor input to quickly cancel an arm warning if required.
Determines the system response that will be triggered if area input activity is detected while the
- The arm warning will be cancelled and the area will be temporarily disarmed/defer armed for a period of time (specified by the area's
field) after which it will begin to auto-arm again.
- The arm warning will be cancelled and the area will be permanently disarmed until it is re-armed by a user or a system action.

If enabled, the system will automatically isolate unsealed inputs in this area during the pre-arm test, rather than failing the arm if armed via a reader or a remote, or prompting a user to seal or isolate them if arming from an LCD Terminal / web interface. These inputs will remain isolated until either the input returns to a secure state, or the area is disarmed.

page is enabled, any module health issues or alarms comms issues in the system will also be included in the pre-arm test. These issues would be automatically overidden and remain overidden until the area disarms.
Note that inputs within the exit path (inputs with
input types) are excluded from the initial pre-arm input test and as such this option will not auto-isolate these inputs. The
Auto-Isolate Unsealed Exit Path Inputs on Exit
option below can be used to auto-isolate these inputs.

This option should only be used after careful consideration, as it is a trade off between convenience for security and may be undesirable. For example, a door or window could be left open or someone could be inside, with the area armed.
Auto-Isolate Unsealed Exit Path Inputs On Exit

If enabled, inputs on the exit path will auto-isolate after the exit delay ends. If the input returns to a secure state or the area is disarmed, the isolated input will automatically de-isolate. Inputs on the exit path are not included in the initial pre-arm input test when arming the area, this is commonly used to avoid faulty inputs or inputs that are delayed in entering a secure state from causing alarms to sound. The inputs that were late to secure would also automatically de-isolate once secure.

Note, this option should only be used after careful consideration, as it is a trade off between convenience for security and may be undesirable. Although it can prevent faulty inputs that are stuck in alarm or inputs that are slow to secure from sounding sirens, it could mean that the exit door could be left unsecure. In addition, the input or inputs assigned the
process group could be isolated and if not secured, then the next user to open the door wouldn't trigger entry delay and instead the alarms would sound.

resources.string: When the area is armed it will monitor the state of the inputs. When one of the inputs added to the area is triggered, depending on the process group the area may activate sirens and siren outputs, send alarm messages to a monitoring stations etc.
Assign outputs to be triggered when the area's siren(s) sound.
When the siren sounds the selected outputs will be turned on.
This checkbox enables and disables the automated action. This can be used to temporarily disable the action.

The trigger condition that determines when this automated action is true or false. When the trigger condition changes from false to true, a set of actions can be automatically executed. Similarly, when the trigger condition changes from true to false, a different set of actions can be automatically executed.

The trigger condition can use the state of any area, door, input, output or time period and will create a trigger statement that is either true or false. For example, a trigger statement could be that the
Door Front Door is not Locked

. Groups can also be created, linking multiple statements or even other groups together. All of these statements and groups will combine together and the trigger condition will still be either true or false.
Groups can be processed in one of two ways:

string.name: F79BA51523CB838A060350F8846E7E59
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "TriggerGroupType" entries

resources.string: True when ALL of the following are true
- This group is true when ALL of the trigger statements and groups in this group are true, otherwise it is false.

string.name: 73267CE9291DCADC2BF2A57DD569BE38
string.notes: Must be unique amongst all "TriggerGroupType" entries

resources.string: True when ANY of the following are true
- This group is true when ANY one of the trigger statements and groups in this group are true, otherwise it is false.

As the areas, doors, inputs, outputs and time periods change state in the system, the trigger condition is continually tested and when it changes from true to false or false to true, the correct set of actions are executed.
This allows both simple and complex automatic responses to be created, potentially executing a response only if multiple items in the system are in the correct state.

Determines what action or actions to perform when the trigger condition changes from false to true. These actions are edge-based and allow for the control of many things (outputs, areas, doors, inputs, readers, etc).

Determines what action or actions to perform when the trigger condition changes from true to false. These actions are edge-based and allow for the control of many things (outputs, areas, doors, inputs, readers, etc).

resources.string: Adds a specific date when this calendar should be valid, that can be repeated annually. This can be used to create single day exceptions such as public holidays.
To add additional single dates, click the
button, this will add a blank single date item.
Adds a range of dates when this calendar should be valid, that can be repeated annually. This can be used to create a date range exception such as school holidays.
To add additional date ranges, click the
button, this will add a blank date range item.

Sets a calendar event that does not fit the single date or date range format. This can be used to create a calculated date exception for when this calendar should be valid, such as the second Tuesday of every month.
To add additional calculated dates, click the
button, this will add a blank calculated date’s item.
The total number of bits present in the Card's data.

The type of credential determines how the system will categorise and process credential data that matches this Credential Template. If you are using Dual or Triple Credential Authentication to control door access, you will need to create enough Credential Templates of different types in order to satisfy the requirements for door access. For example: for a door configured with Dual Credential Authentication, you might require that a unique Card and Biometric - Fingerprint credential both need to be presented by a user before they are granted access through the door.

resources.string: The template type determines how the system will store and use the credential data.
fields will be visible below. When assigning a credential to a user, you will first select the template and then enter the credential number.
fields will not be visible below. When assigning a credential to a user, you will first select the template, badge the credential at a reader and copy the raw credential data from the
Select the card format that matches the cards you will be using. If more than one card format is being used, additional credential templates will need to be created.
If you do not know what format your cards are, open the
page and badge a card at a reader. The latest
Credential Read Failed because Site Code was Unknown
message will contain the card format.

If the card format required is not available, Inner Range may be able to supply an upgrade file for a fee to allow your Inception system to support the custom cards. Pricing and availability is subject to Inner Range discretion.
The site code that is programmed on the cards being used. If more than one site code is being used, additional credential templates will need to be created.
If you do not know what the site code the cards are, open the
Card Read Failed because Site Code was Unknown
message will contain the site code.

resources.string: Chooses the method of connection that this Connection item should utilise. For a more detailed explanation of how the different Connection Types work, please see the Connections page description at the top of this page.

- The Connection sends and receives data over a specified serial port. Serial ports can be used with USB to Serial converters and multiple ports can be achieved with a USB Hub. This connection type is best suited for when bidirectional communication is required between Inception and an external system.

- The Connection acts as a TCP client and attempts to open a connection to a specified IP adress and port. This connection type is best suited for instances where the Inception device is the one responsible for initiating the connection and sending messages to an external system.

- The Connection acts as a TCP server and listens for incoming connections on a specified port. This connection type is best suited for instances where the external system is the one responsible for initiating the connection to the Inception device and sending messages.
- The Connection sends HTTP requests to a server at a specified URL/IP/hostname, and the
sent through the connection are treated as the end of the URL and/or the query string. For example: with the Connection's URL set to
results in the Connection sending a GET request to the URL

. HTTP Connections can only send messages, no messages can be received. HTTP Connections can optionally use Basic or Digest authentication for APIs that require valid user credentials. HTTP Send Connections cannot be used to receive messages, a separate HTTP (Receive) connection must be created to do this.

- The Connection receives HTTP requests made to a specific endpoint on the Inception web server (/receive_http_connection), with a URL query parameter (key) to identify which connection the message is intended for by matching on the Key String field. HTTP Receive Connections can receive messages either from GET requests containing text in the

to send a message to a connection with a Key string of example), or POST requests containing text in the request body. HTTP Receive connections can optionally require a username and password for HTTP Basic authentication (Digest authentication is not supported for receiving).

resources.string: Determines the serial port that the Connection should try and connect to. Each detected serial port will be listed, along with whether it is currently plugged in and if it has already been assigned to something else in the system.
Unplugging serial ports and refreshing the page will update the
state, allowing an easy way of identifying serial ports.
will cause the Connection to attempt to connect to the first plugged-in and unassigned serial device.

The rate at which to send/receive data to and from the chosen serial port. Some serial devices only support certain baud rates, check with the device's manufacturer to confirm which rates are supported.
The number of data bits to use for the serial connection (default is 8).
The parity bit setting to use for the serial connection (default is None).
The number of stop bits to use for the serial connection (default is 1).
The TCP port of the server that this Connection should open a TCP connection to.
For client connections, valid port numbers are between 1 and 65535 (inclusive).
For server connections, valid port numbers are between 1025 and 65535 (inclusive).
The IP address of the server that this Connection should open a TCP connection to.
